that your days may be long on the earth.<|26.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Throughout history, the Ten Commandments has been the punchline of jokes, the subject of fanciful Hollywood films, namely the one with Charlton Heston as Moses, and the center of controversy of various groups' fights over displays of religious imagery on publicly funded land.<|23.10|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Before the Ten Commandments was the lightning rod it is today.<|3.86|> <|4.00|> It was an ethical and communal vision handed over to a fledgling community of formerly enslaved people.<|12.16|> <|12.40|> Not simply an arbitrary list of rules composed to make us feel ashamed for the ways that we exploit ourselves, each other, or God.<|24.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The Exodus text has a curious reminder from its outset.<|5.58|> <|6.58|> I am the God who brought you out of Egypt, out of the house of enslavement.<|11.52|> <|12.04|> Over the past three weeks in Lent, we have heard three separate but related accounts of God's faithfulness.<|19.82|> <|20.66|> First to Noah and his family in the ark.<|23.96|> <|24.38|> Then to Sarah and her family, and to Sarah and her family.<|28.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And now to their descendants, the people of Israel.<|5.18|> <|5.66|> For some 400 years, the descendants of Jacob, who was nicknamed Israel, toiled under the oppressive regimes of the pharaohs.<|16.18|> <|16.74|> The book of Exodus begins by saying that Israel's oppression in Egypt was rooted in the amnesia of a pharaoh who did not remember Joseph.<|27.74|> <|27.74|> Joseph.<|28.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The ancestor of the Israelites who as an Egyptian government official caused the nation to flourish.<|8.96|> <|9.18|> And you might remember this story from the latter part of the book of institutional commodification.<|8.66|> <|8.66|> The reduction of the Israelites to nothing more than property to be owned,<|14.10|> <|14.58|> labor to be exploited, and a people to be dehumanized.<|19.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Through a series of plagues and signs attributed to Israel's God, liberation from Pharaoh's tyrannical rule was slowly realized.<|9.82|> <|10.92|> By the time we meet this ragtag band of recently freed people, they are in the middle of the wilderness, ambivalent about their newfound freedom.<|20.98|> <|20.98|> Amen.<|22.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> As a priest and as a person, I hear story after story from people who have been weighed down either by disease, abuse, or some other millstone, who then suddenly find themselves in remission and<|19.40|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> or free from that abusive relationship or forgiven of an insurmountable debt.<|7.40|> <|7.72|> And the question people often are left with when they realize they aren't going to die from that particular disease<|16.26|> <|16.26|> or be stuck in a cycle of abuse is what do I do now that I'm free?<|22.50|> <|22.50|> I'm free.<|23.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Now that I've left that person or community that was holding me back, what do I do now that I've reached this surprising chapter in my life?<|10.32|> <|11.50|> That is where the people of Israel find themselves in their narrative of liberation and wilderness wandering.<|18.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> What do we do and where do we go now that we are no longer enslaved by a dehumanizing regime preoccupied with productivity and domination?<|11.74|> <|13.68|> If the Ten Commandments is anything, it is a social vision of gentleness, rest, and compassion that emerges in response to the exploitative economy of Pharaoh.<|27.70|> <|27.70|> Pharaoh.<|28.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It is a hint of a world put to rights by a loving and liberating God, which is why the Sabbath command comes as no surprise.<|13.02|> <|14.48|> Remember the Sabbath day and keep it holy.<|17.00|> <|17.96|> For in six days, the Lord made heaven and earth, but rested the seventh day.<|23.08|> <|23.08|> Amen.<|24.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Pharaoh might have demanded nonstop, back-breaking, soul-suppressing labor every day of every week.<|8.88|> <|9.26|> But the God of the Exodus, the God of the oppressed and the marginalized and the forgotten and forsaken,<|17.30|> <|17.30|> this God is more concerned with Israel's humanity than with its productivity.<|23.02|> <|23.74|> Amen.<|24.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In the wilderness, God reminds Israel of the origins of the world and creation itself.<|7.82|> <|8.84|> Taking them back to those formative Genesis narratives.<|12.86|> <|14.28|> Recalling for them not only God's rhythm of work and rest, but the gift of rest and work that is theirs by virtue of God's chain-breaking power.<|26.54|> <|27.38|> Amen.<|28.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> No longer will you be defined by what you make or how fast you make it, God says in this commandment.<|8.56|> <|8.56|> From now on, you will be defined by your restored humanity, your reclaimed dignity.<|17.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> According to theologian Walter Brueggemann, Israel rests because God rests.<|6.08|> <|7.20|> This God is not a workaholic.<|10.38|> <|11.34|> God has no need to be more secure, more sufficient, more in control, or more noticed.<|19.12|> <|19.12|> He is not a workaholic.<|20.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It is ordained in the very fabric of creation that the world is not a place of endless productivity or ambition.<|8.90|> <|9.90|> In other words, we were made to work and rest, to work and play.<|21.00|> <|21.00|> To work and play.<|21.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> To be human.<|1.70|> <|2.78|> Not to return emails on our days off.<|6.56|> <|7.06|> I'm preaching to myself.<|8.50|> <|11.90|> To neglect taking vacation and mental health days when possible.<|16.90|> invitation to take in the God who is closer than our next breath.<|8.90|> <|9.74|> The God who offers Israel and by extension us an alternative to Pharaoh's way of running the world.<|16.84|> Israel is told to put no other gods before the living God,<|15.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> To refrain from making idols, to abstain from desecrating God's name, to honor
